summary: The author developed a 4th place solution for an ECG digitization competition by prioritizing robust image rectification and regression-based waveform estimation, treating signal segmentation as an auxiliary task. Key ideas include detecting grid intersections and layout to warp images into a normalized coordinate system, vertically splitting the rectified image into aligned segments to reduce prediction complexity, and using an SNR-based loss function aligned with the evaluation metric. The pipeline leverages external ECG image datasets and a custom U-Net architecture with asymmetric decoding for efficient temporal resolution.
extensive_summary:
## Solution Overview
The author achieved 4th place by designing a pipeline that prioritizes robust image rectification and regression-based waveform estimation, treating signal segmentation as an auxiliary task. The core idea is to simplify the prediction task by mapping ECG images to a normalized coordinate system and vertically splitting them into aligned segments.

## Processing Pipeline
The pipeline operates in four stages. First, a sliding window (704x704, 0.5 overlap) feeds a multi-task model to detect grid intersections and layout segmentation (lead separators and calibration signals). Orientation is corrected by rotating the image to a unified upright position. Second, the corrected image is resized and padded to 1536x1536, and a model predicts vertical and horizontal line indices for each pixel. Intersections are assigned row/column indices via majority voting in a 6px neighborhood. Third, grid unwarping maps the image to a fixed 1700x2200 coordinate system, followed by cropping the top 25% to remove headers. The image is then vertically split into 4 segments, each resized to 800x800, aligning the time axis and enabling cross-lead information interpolation.

## Models & Training Strategy
Stages 1 and 2 use a ConvNeXt Small backbone (`convnext_small.dinov3_lvd1689m`) pre-trained with FCMAE in the ECG image domain, which accelerated convergence and improved detection with limited annotations. Stage 4 employs a modified U-Net architecture featuring an asymmetric decoder that switches to horizontal-only upsampling after reaching 1/8 scale to save computation while preserving temporal resolution. Multi-head regression outputs short lead waveforms via vertical global average pooling and 1D convolutions, while the long Lead II is generated by concatenating segment features along the time axis. An auxiliary segmentation head discriminates signal areas. The loss function explicitly optimizes SNR and Mean terms separately to align with the evaluation metric. External datasets (PTB-XL, CODE-15%) were converted to images using `ecg-image-kit` to train the auxiliary task.

## Ensembling & Results
Multiple models with different backbones were combined using a weighted median ensemble, which proved more robust against spike-like outliers than the mean. The final submission leveraged linear interpolation to resample fixed-length outputs to the test data's sampling frequency.
competition_flow: Raw ECG images are processed through a four-stage pipeline involving grid detection, coordinate indexing, rectification, and vertical splitting, followed by a regression model that estimates time-series waveforms which are resampled and ensembled.
data_processing: Sliding window (704x704, 0.5 overlap) for grid detection
Grid unwarping to fixed 1700x2200 coordinate system
Linear interpolation resampling based on test fs
models: U-Net based architecture with asymmetric decoder
Multi-head regression (short leads head, long lead II head, auxiliary segmentation head)
loss_functions: SNR-based loss (optimized SNR term and Mean term separately)
ensembling: Multiple models with different backbones were combined using a weighted median ensemble, which proved more robust against spike-like outlier predictions than the mean.
insights: Vertical splitting reduces prediction complexity by aligning the time axis within segments and enabling cross-lead information interpolation.
Using an SNR-based loss function closely aligns the training objective with the competition's evaluation metric.
Pretraining with FCMAE in the ECG image domain accelerates convergence and improves detection accuracy even with limited labeled data.
critical_findings: The median ensemble outperformed the mean ensemble in handling spike-like outlier predictions.
Switching to horizontal-only upsampling after 1/8 scale in the decoder significantly reduces computational cost while preserving high temporal resolution.

papers.title: Convnext v2: Co-designing and scaling convnets with masked autoencoders
ECG-image-kit: a synthetic image generation toolbox to facilitate deep learning-based electrocardiogram digitization
ECG-Image-Kit: A Toolkit for Synthesis, Analysis, and Digitization of Electrocardiogram Images
PTB-XL, a large publicly available electrocardiography dataset (version 1.0.3)
CODE-15%: a large scale annotated dataset of 12-lead ECGs (1.0.0)
